summary:
Nadia Khasyshyn worked primarily as a computer-vision and machine-learning engineer supporting an embedded detection and tracking system, with significant contributions to surrounding infrastructure and tooling.

A core focus was the detection/CV pipeline: building a system for effective model evaluation, automatic hyperparameter tuning and pipeline optimization (including via RayTune), iterating a classifier on a new 40k dataset, validating datasets with alternative models, and adapting optical-flow code. She also handled object tracking work, including a distance-dependent tracker invalidator, frame-rate discrepancies between tracker nodes, and OSD reporting of tracker resets.

Data and annotation tooling formed another theme: spatial labeling with Gemini, establishing and debugging daily labeling processes, labeling thermal data based on RGB, uploading daily annotations to Label Studio, automating dataset notifications, and fixing metric synchronization.

She did substantial OS/infra and build work around USB flash provisioning—toggling obfuscation, reducing build verbosity, validating and preserving Godot file hashes across restarts, and rebranding system messaging.

Additional work covered system reliability and performance, including PiCamera restart handling, reducing main.py CPU usage, debug tile saving, mission continuation after thermal-filter attack resets, battery-usage prediction for path traversal, and processor-efficient OSD for azimuth display.

Bohdan Forostianyi worked primarily on flight control software for an autonomous UAV/drone platform, building on the ArduPilot/MAVLink ecosystem. A significant portion of his work centered on flight control logic and stabilization, including manual altitude control without exiting stabilization, azimuth stabilization during descent and landing, manual control of maximum azimuth angle in flight, and handling servo movement and throttle deadzone parameters during azimuth operations.

He also implemented failsafe and safety mechanisms, including general failsafe protection and fixing voltage-based failsafe triggering after takeoff, as well as resolving issues with mode changes to Guided under a custom flight mode. Additional work involved parameter abstraction layers for configuration handling.

A second focus area was tooling and integration, particularly developing and supporting Mission Planner plugins for configuration updates and code activation, along with MAVLink Tunnel communication support.

He further contributed to OS/infrastructure and security work, including running code from OS memory and implementing a second layer of encryption over radio communications.

Overall, his work spans flight control and stabilization, failsafe/safety systems, ground-station tooling (Mission Planner plugins), and low-level OS and communications security.

Danylo Tomashy worked primarily on embedded flight control and computer vision systems for an autonomous aerial platform, spanning several interconnected themes.

A major focus was navigation resilience and failsafe behavior, including compass-based return when radio beacons are lost, detecting beacon glitches as a trigger for Failsafe mode, position-holding return on glitch detection, and continued flight ("Sine Failsafe") during beacon loss within a mission. He also implemented Smart Takeoff and extended missions to account for predicted displacement after descent.

In the targeting and tracking domain, he handled attack termination logic when tracking of a designated object is lost (reverting to mission), addressing related mission-state transitions.

He did significant work on camera and imaging subsystems, including thermal camera code adaptation, automatic thermal offset calibration using a warm object, synchronization of thermal and daytime video streams, and evaluating the thermal camera's SDK capabilities.

Additional tasks covered firmware/data-handling issues, such as fixing updated code not loading from a flash drive into the system, and correcting video recording that was not properly toggled via an RC channel.

Overall, his work combined flight control logic, navigation/failsafe robustness, thermal and visual camera integration, and low-level system/firmware fixes.

Volodymyr Pavliukevych worked primarily on embedded OS and infrastructure for drone systems, focusing on boot reliability, build performance, and secure provisioning on Raspberry Pi hardware.

A significant portion of his work centered on operating system stability and boot behavior, including diagnosing and fixing cases where the OS or autopilot failed to load after restarts or USB flash boots, resolving a device descriptor error encountered in field conditions, and addressing intermittent flight controller detection issues.

He also drove Secure Boot initiatives, investigating and implementing an RPI Secure Boot Provisioner and enabling Secure Boot for an OS variant supporting Hailo acceleration.

In parallel, he improved tooling and system performance, notably reducing flash drive build time from 3–6 minutes toward 1–2 minutes and minimizing emulator kill time.

His work on the MAVLink/flight-control stack included refactoring the fc_mavlink_state_listener to use a Redis pub/sub model instead of polling with redis.get, resolving a crash during metrics synchronization, and fixing a startup conflict between mavlink-router and the DNS service.

Overall, his focus spanned OS/infrastructure reliability, secure boot provisioning, build and runtime tooling optimization, and flight-control messaging components.
